docs.title: Veeam Health Check
docs.text:
Generate comprehensive configuration reports for your Veeam environment in minutes.

Community Tool

This is a community-supported tool from VeeamHub and is not an officially supported Veeam product. It does not phone home or communicate with anything beyond your Veeam infrastructure components.

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
Veeam Health Check is a lightweight Windows utility that analyzes your Veeam Backup & Replication (VBR) or Veeam Backup for Microsoft 365 (VB365) installation and produces a detailed, single-page HTML report covering:

Area Details Job Session Analytics Min/max/average for duration, backup size, data size, and wait times Success & Change Rates Environment-wide visibility across all jobs Concurrency Heat Maps Job and task overlap visualized over time Configuration Review Highlights areas for potential improvement Curated Guidance Best practices, recommendations, and links to relevant documentation

docs.title: Export Formats
docs.text:
• HTML — single-file report with embedded CSS and JavaScript
  • PDF — via DinkToPdf
  • PowerPoint — via HtmlToOpenXml
  • Scrubbed Mode — anonymizes IPs, server names, and credentials before sharing

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: The Veeam Health Check tool can now export reports as PowerPoint presentations, making it easier to share findings in meetings and presentations.

docs.title: Option 1: Command Line
docs.text:
Add the /pptx flag when running the tool:

# For VBR (Veeam Backup & Replication)
.\VeeamHealthCheck.exe /vbr /pptx

# For VB365 (Veeam Backup for Microsoft 365)
.\VeeamHealthCheck.exe /vb365 /pptx

# Export both PDF and PowerPoint
.\VeeamHealthCheck.exe /vbr /pdf /pptx

docs.title: Option 2: GUI
docs.text:
1. Launch VeeamHealthCheck.exe
  2. In the Export Options section, check ☑ Export PowerPoint
  3. Run your health check as normal

docs.title: What Gets Exported
docs.text: The PowerPoint export includes: - Title Slide: Overview of the health check report - Table Slides: Each HTML table becomes one or more PowerPoint slides - Section Titles: Preserved from the HTML report

docs.title: Example Slides
docs.text:
• License Summary
  • Backup Server & Config DB Info
  • Security Summary
  • Job Summary
  • Protected Workloads
  • Proxy Info
  • Repository Details
  • And more...

docs.title: Table Pagination
docs.text:
Large tables are automatically split for readability:

Before (30+ rows): 

License Summary
[30+ rows of data - hard to read on one slide]

After (automatic pagination): 

Slide 1: License Summary (1/3)
[Rows 1-30]

Slide 2: License Summary (2/3)
[Rows 31-60]

Slide 3: License Summary (3/3)
[Rows 61-90]

docs.title: Output Location
docs.text:
PowerPoint files are saved in the same location as your HTML reports:

Unscrubbed Reports: 

C:\VeeamHealthCheckReports\UNSCRUBBED\
  Veeam Health Check Report_VBR_ServerName_2024.12.09.183000.html
  Veeam Health Check Report_VBR_ServerName_2024.12.09.183000.pptx  ← PowerPoint file
  Veeam Health Check Report_VBR_ServerName_2024.12.09.183000.pdf   ← (if /pdf also used)

Scrubbed Reports: 

C:\VeeamHealthCheckReports\SCRUBBED\
  Veeam Health Check Report_VBR_abc1234_2024.12.09.183000.html
  Veeam Health Check Report_VBR_abc1234_2024.12.09.183000.pptx

docs.title: Customizing the PowerPoint
docs.text: After export, you can: 1. Apply your organization's PowerPoint template 2. Add company logos and branding 3. Adjust colors and fonts 4. Add presenter notes 5. Reorder slides for your audience

docs.title: Using in Meetings
docs.text: The PowerPoint format is ideal for: - Executive briefings - Technical reviews - Audit presentations - Status updates - Change advisory board meetings

docs.title: Recommended Workflow
docs.text:
1. Run health check with /pptx export
  2. Open the generated .pptx file
  3. Review all slides and remove any you don't need
  4. Add title slide with your company branding
  5. Add summary/conclusion slides
  6. Save as final presentation

docs.title: Fonts
docs.text:
• Slide Titles: 28pt, Bold
  • Table Headers: 14pt, Bold
  • Table Data: 11pt, Regular

docs.title: Special Characters
docs.text: These are preserved in the export: - ✓ Checkmarks (success/enabled) - ☑ Checked boxes - ☐ Unchecked boxes - ⚠ Warning symbols

docs.title: Table Structure
docs.text:
• Native PowerPoint tables (not images)
  • Editable after export
  • Can be resized and reformatted
  • Copy-paste friendly

docs.title: No .pptx file generated
docs.text:
• Check that you used the /pptx flag or checked the GUI option
  • Verify you're not using scrubbed mode (scrubbed exports skip PPTX by design)
  • Look for error messages in the console output

docs.title: Slides look crowded
docs.text:
• This is expected for tables with many columns
  • After export, rotate slide orientation to landscape if needed
  • Consider splitting wide tables manually in PowerPoint

docs.title: Missing data
docs.text:
• Ensure all sections were expanded in the HTML (this happens automatically)
  • Check the HTML report first to confirm data is present there

docs.title: Comparing Export Formats
docs.text:
Feature HTML PDF PowerPoint Interactive ✓ ✗ ✗ Searchable ✓ ✓ ✓ Editable ✗ ✗ ✓ Presentation-ready ✗ △ ✓ Print-friendly △ ✓ ✓ Meeting-friendly ✗ △ ✓

Recommendation: Generate all three formats for maximum flexibility! 

.\VeeamHealthCheck.exe /vbr /pdf /pptx

docs.title: Custom Output Path
docs.text:
Combine with the /path parameter: 

.\VeeamHealthCheck.exe /vbr /pptx /path="D:\Reports"

docs.title: Scheduled Exports
docs.text:
Create a scheduled task that generates weekly PowerPoint reports: 

$trigger = New-ScheduledTaskTrigger -Weekly -DaysOfWeek Monday -At 6am
$action = New-ScheduledTaskAction -Execute "C:\Tools\VeeamHealthCheck.exe" -Argument "/vbr /pptx"
Register-ScheduledTask -TaskName "Weekly Veeam Report" -Trigger $trigger -Action $action

docs.title: Support
docs.text: For issues or questions about PowerPoint export: 1. Check the main README.md in the repository 2. Review the POWERPOINT_EXPORT_SUMMARY.md for technical details 3. Submit an issue on the GitHub repository

docs.title: Version Information
docs.text:
PowerPoint export feature added in version 3.0+

Requires: - .NET 8.0 or higher - Windows 7 or higher - PowerPoint not required (files can be opened in Google Slides, LibreOffice Impress, etc.)

docs.title: Current Status
docs.text: Our CI/CD pipeline scans releases with VirusTotal (76+ antivirus engines). We occasionally see false positives from less common AV vendors.

docs.title: Latest Results
docs.text:
• Detection Rate: 1/76 engines (Zillya)
  • All Major Vendors: ✅ Clean (Microsoft, Kaspersky, Sophos, McAfee, Symantec, etc.)
  • Assessment: False positive due to unsigned single-file .NET executable

docs.title: Why False Positives Occur
docs.text:
1. Single-File Executable: .NET's single-file publish uses self-extraction
  2. No Code Signing: Unsigned binaries trigger heuristic detections
  3. Compression: Heavy compression can look suspicious
  4. PowerShell Integration: Tool executes PowerShell scripts internally

docs.title: Current Threshold Policy
docs.text:
The workflow allows up to 2 detections from obscure AV engines: - 0 detections: ✅ Pass (ideal) - 1-2 detections: ⚠️ Warning + Pass (likely false positive) - 3+ detections: ❌ Fail (needs investigation)

This prevents obscure AVs like Zillya from blocking legitimate releases while still catching real issues.

docs.title: 1. Code Signing Certificate (Most Effective) ⭐
docs.text:
Benefits: - Dramatically reduces false positives (typically to 0) - Builds user trust - Required for some enterprise environments - Windows SmartScreen won't block downloads

Options: - Standard Code Signing: ~$100-200/year (e.g., Sectigo, DigiCert) - EV Code Signing: ~$300-500/year (includes hardware token, immediate trust)

Implementation: 

# Sign the executable before packaging
signtool sign /f certificate.pfx /p password /tr http://timestamp.digicert.com /td sha256 /fd sha256 "VeeamHealthCheck.exe"

GitHub Actions Integration: 

- name: Import code signing certificate
  run: |
    $pfxBytes = [Convert]::FromBase64String("${{ secrets.CODE_SIGNING_CERT }}")
    [IO.File]::WriteAllBytes("cert.pfx", $pfxBytes)

- name: Sign executable
  run: |
    & "C:\Program Files (x86)\Windows Kits\10\bin\10.0.22621.0\x64\signtool.exe" sign `
      /f cert.pfx `
      /p "${{ secrets.CERT_PASSWORD }}" `
      /tr http://timestamp.digicert.com `
      /td sha256 `
      /fd sha256 `
      "publish/out/VeeamHealthCheck.exe"

docs.title: 2. Submit False Positive Reports
docs.text:
When a false positive occurs, submit to the vendor:

  • Zillya: https://zillya.com/support/false-positive
  • Most vendors: VirusTotal has a "Request re-analysis" feature

docs.title: 3. Alternative Publishing Method
docs.text:
Instead of single-file publish, use framework-dependent with separate DLLs:

<!-- In .csproj -->
<PublishSingleFile>false</PublishSingleFile>
<SelfContained>false</SelfContained>
<RuntimeIdentifier>win-x64</RuntimeIdentifier>

Pros: - Fewer false positives - Smaller download size

Cons: - Requires .NET 8 runtime on target machine - Multiple files to manage

docs.title: 4. Windows Defender Scan (Pre-check)
docs.text:
Add a Windows Defender scan before VirusTotal:

- name: Scan with Windows Defender
  run: |
    $exePath = "publish/out/VeeamHealthCheck.exe"
    & 'C:\Program Files\Windows Defender\MpCmdRun.exe' -Scan -ScanType 3 -File $exePath

    if ($LASTEXITCODE -eq 0) {
      Write-Host "✅ Windows Defender: Clean"
    } else {
      Write-Host "❌ Windows Defender detected a threat"
      exit 1
    }

docs.title: Short Term (Current)
docs.text: ✅ Accept 1-2 false positives from obscure AVs ✅ Monitor major vendors (all must be clean) ✅ Document scan results in releases

docs.title: Medium Term (Next 3 months)
docs.text:
• [ ] Investigate code signing certificate options
  • [ ] Submit false positives to vendors for whitelisting
  • [ ] Add Windows Defender pre-check

docs.title: Long Term (Next 6 months)
docs.text:
• [ ] Obtain EV code signing certificate
  • [ ] Implement automated signing in CI/CD

docs.title: Verification for Users
docs.text: Users can verify downloads independently:

docs.title: Check VirusTotal
docs.text:
# Get file hash
$hash = (Get-FileHash "VeeamHealthCheck-X.X.X.zip" -Algorithm SHA256).Hash

# Visit https://www.virustotal.com/gui/home/search
# Paste the hash to see scan results

docs.title: Windows Defender Scan
docs.text: & 'C:\Program Files\Windows Defender\MpCmdRun.exe' -Scan -ScanType 3 -File "VeeamHealthCheck.exe"

docs.title: Verify Release Hash
docs.text:
$expected = "HASH_FROM_RELEASE_NOTES"
$actual = (Get-FileHash "VeeamHealthCheck-X.X.X.zip" -Algorithm SHA256).Hash

if ($expected -eq $actual) {
  Write-Host "✅ Hash verified - file is authentic"
} else {
  Write-Host "❌ Hash mismatch - file may be compromised"
}

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: We welcome contributions — bug reports, fixes, new features, and documentation improvements.

docs.title: How to Contribute
docs.text: All contributions to this repository must be signed as described in the Developer Certificate of Origin. Your signature certifies that you wrote the contribution or have the right to pass it on as an open source contribution.

docs.title: Report a Bug or Request a Feature
docs.text: Open a new issue — it's that easy.

docs.title: Submit a Fix or Feature
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository
  2. Create a feature branch from master
  3. Write your change with tests where applicable
  4. Follow the test naming convention: [MethodUnderTest]_[Scenario]_[ExpectedBehavior]
  5. Open a pull request against master

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text:
# Restore dependencies
dotnet restore vHC/HC.sln

# Build (debug)
dotnet build vHC/HC.sln --configuration Debug

# Run tests (Windows only — requires WPF/.NET Windows)
dotnet test vHC/VhcXTests/VhcXTests.csproj

Note

Tests require Windows due to WPF dependencies. Non-Windows builds skip test compilation.

docs.title: Commit Convention
docs.text: Use Conventional Commits — feat:, fix:, chore:, ci:, test:, docs:. Feature commits (feat:) appear in the auto-generated Feature Timeline.

docs.title: Export Formats
docs.text: Format Notes HTML Default — single file, embedded CSS/JS, works offline PDF Via DinkToPdf, print-ready PowerPoint Via HtmlToOpenXml, editable slide deck Scrubbed Any format with IPs, server names, and credentials anonymized

docs.title: Execution Modes
docs.text: Mode Flag Use Case GUI /gui Interactive, guided configuration CLI /run Scripted, automated, unattended Silent /run /silent Fleet automation, no prompts Remote /run /host=<name> Run from a jump host against a remote VBR server Security /security Security-focused assessment only Import /import Generate report from pre-collected CSV data

docs.title: Platform Coverage
docs.text:
Veeam Health Check detects and reports on managed server platform types:

  • Proxmox VE
  • Nutanix AHV
  • HPE Morpheus VME
  • Scale Computing HyperCore
  • XCP-ng
  • Sangfor HCI
  • Red Hat Virtualization
  • Kasten
  • Standard VMware/Hyper-V

docs.title: Silent / Unattended Mode
docs.text:
Added in May 2026. Enables fleet-wide automation with no interactive prompts.

VeeamHealthCheck.exe /run /silent /days:30 /outdir=\\nas\reports\$env:COMPUTERNAME /pdf

Exit codes are documented in the help menu (/help).

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Windows system with VBR Console or VB365 installed
  • Run as an elevated user with Backup Administrator role
  • 500 MB free disk space on C:\ (default output: C:\temp\vHC)
  • Veeam Cloud Service Provider servers are not supported

docs.title: Supported Versions
docs.text: Product Supported Versions Notes Veeam Backup & Replication v12.3, v13 (Windows & Linux) For v11/v12 pre-12.3, use Health Check v2 Veeam Backup for Microsoft 365 v6, v7, v8

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
1. Download the latest VeeamHealthCheck.zip
  2. Extract the archive on your Veeam server
  3. Run VeeamHealthCheck.exe as Administrator

No installer. No dependencies to install. Single executable.

docs.title: Running a Health Check
docs.text:
GUICLI
  1. Launch VeeamHealthCheck.exe as Administrator
  2. Configure options (reporting window, export format, output path)
  3. Accept the terms and click RUN
  4. Review the generated report
# Standard health check (7-day window)
VeeamHealthCheck.exe /run

# 30-day window, also export PDF
VeeamHealthCheck.exe /run /days:30 /pdf

# Custom output directory, open report when done
VeeamHealthCheck.exe /run /outdir=D:\Reports /show:report

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text:
VeeamHealthCheck.exe [options]
Option Description /run Execute health check via CLI /gui Launch graphical interface /help Show full help menu /days:<N> Reporting window: 7, 12, 30, or 90 days (default: 7) /outdir=<path> Output directory (default: C:\temp\vHC) /pdf Also export as PDF /pptx Also export as PowerPoint /scrub:true Anonymize sensitive data /lite Skip per-job HTML exports (faster) /show:report Open report in browser when done /show:files Open output folder in Explorer /remote Enable remote execution /host=<hostname> Target remote Veeam server /security Run security-focused assessment only /import[:<path>] Generate report from existing CSV data /clearcreds Clear stored credentials /debug Enable debug logging

docs.title: Remote Execution
docs.text:
Run against a remote Veeam server without being locally logged into it:

VeeamHealthCheck.exe /run /host=vbrserver.veeam.local

Credentials are prompted and stored securely in Windows Credential Manager.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Problem Solution "Access Denied" Run as Administrator with Backup Administrator role "No Veeam installation detected" Tool must run on a system with VBR Console or VB365 installed Low disk space errors Ensure C:\ has at least 500 MB free PowerShell errors Verify PowerShell 7+ is installed Credentials not working Run /clearcreds then re-authenticate
